title: Proof that the Klein-Gordon type equation with alpha attractor potential has no Liouvillian solution or as a composition of special functions
abstract: This study investigates the analytical solvability of the Klein-Gordon and Duffin-Kemmer-Petiau (DKP) equations for a scalar particle interacting with a transcendental $α$-attractor-type potential, $V(x) = V_0 e^{a \tanh(bx)}$. We first address the problem of integrability within the framework of Picard-Vessiot theory. By analyzing the differential field extensions associated with the system, we demonstrated that the differential Galois group is the full special linear group $SL(2, \mathbb{C})$. Given that this group is not solvable, we provide rigorous proof for the non-existence of Liouvillian solutions, effectively ruling out any expression in terms of primitives and elementary functions. Building upon this result, we further establish that wavefunctions cannot be represented as finite compositions or transformations of classical special functions, such as those of the Bessel, Whittaker, or Heun families. This second conclusion is supported by the ``double-transcendence'' of the potential; we prove via the Hermite-Lindemann theorem that no rational coordinate transformation $z(x)$ exists that could map the physical equation into an ordinary differential equations(ODE) with rational coefficients. Consequently, the $α$-attractor potential is strictly non-integrable and lies entirely outside the landscape of solvable relativistic quantum systems.
summary: This theoretical paper proves that the Klein-Gordon equation with an $\alpha$-attractor potential is strictly non-integrable. Using Differential Galois Theory, the authors demonstrate that the system's associated differential equations have a non-solvable Galois group ($SL(2, \mathbb{C})$). This mathematically rules out any possibility of finding solutions using elementary functions or known special functions.
main_problem: The study investigates the analytical solvability (integrability) of a Klein-Gordon type equation featuring an $\alpha$-attractor potential.
main_result: The differential Galois group is found to be $SL(2, \mathbb{C})$, which is non-solvable, proving that no Liouvillian solutions exist and the system cannot be expressed as a composition of classical special functions.
method: Advanced techniques from Differential Galois Theory (Picard-Vessiot theory) were employed to analyze the associated Riccati equation and determine the structure of the differential Galois group.
model_or_system: The model is a scalar particle governed by a Klein-Gordon type equation in one dimension, interacting with the transcendental $\alpha$-attractor potential $V(x) = V_0 e^a \tanh(bx)$.
key_observables: The differential Galois group of the system; the existence or non-existence of Liouvillian solutions.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that solvability in terms of special functions implies the differential Galois group must be solvable.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by transforming the physical equation into a normal form, analyzing the associated Riccati equation using techniques like the Kovacic algorithm, and ultimately determining the structure of the differential Galois group to prove non-integrability.
why_interesting: While focused on relativistic quantum mechanics rather than AMO systems directly, the rigorous application of advanced mathematical tools (Differential Galois Theory) to determine the fundamental solvability limits of a physical model is highly relevant for understanding when complex Hamiltonians can be solved analytically in any field of physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
